### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-n#nasdaq_100_index_ndx)

NASDAQ 100 Index ($NDX)

The NASDAQ 100 Index ($NDX) tracks the 100 largest stocks listed by the Nasdaq Composite Index. The NASDAQ 100 is often treated as an index of “tech stocks” simply because its components are mostly new technology companies.

circle-info

[**Chart $NDX in GalleryView.**arrow-up-right](https://stockcharts.com/gallery?$ndx)

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-n#nasdaq_composite_index_compq)

NASDAQ Composite Index ($COMPQ)

The NASDAQ Composite Index ($COMPQ) is a market capitalization-weighted index of over 5000 stocks. Because it is weighted by [market capitalization](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-m#market_capitalization)
, large companies such as Microsoft, Apple, Google, Cisco, Intel and Amazon dominate the index. With such a large portion of the index dominated by technology stocks, the NASDAQ Composite is more a barometer for the technology sector than the broader market. The name “NASDAQ” is derived from the National Association of Securities Dealers Automated Quotation (System). See the [NASDAQ web sitearrow-up-right](http://business.nasdaq.com/)
 for more information about the NASDAQ Composite.

circle-info

[**Chart $COMPQ in GalleryView.**arrow-up-right](https://stockcharts.com/gallery?$compq)

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-n#negative_directional_indicator_-di)

Negative Directional Indicator (-DI)

When the ADX Indicator is selected, SharpCharts plots the Positive Directional Indicator (+DI), Negative Directional Indicator (-DI), and [Average Directional Index](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/technical-indicators-and-overlays/technical-indicators/average-directional-index-adx)
 (ADX). Within the black, green, and red color scheme on SharpCharts, -DI is the red line that measures the force of the down moves over a set period. The default setting is 14 periods.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-n#negative_reversal)

Negative Reversal

Developed by Andrew Cardwell, a negative reversal occurs when RSI forms a higher high and the security forms a lower high. It is a backward bearish divergence. See our ChartSchool article on [Relative Strength Index (RSI)](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/technical-indicators-and-overlays/technical-indicators/relative-strength-index-rsi)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-n#net_asset_value_nav)

Net Asset Value (NAV)

The dollar value of a single mutual fund share, based on the value of the underlying assets of the fund minus its liabilities, divided by the number of shares outstanding. Calculated at the end of each business day.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-n#net_new_highs)

Net New Highs

Market breadth indicators that can be used to identify strength or weakness behind market moves. Net New Highs are found by subtracting the number of new lows from the number of new highs and are usually calculated for the NYSE, Nasdaq and Amex.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-n#new_highs_and_new_lows)

New Highs and New Lows

New highs refers to the number of stocks recording their highest price level in 52 weeks. New lows are the number of stocks recording their lowest price level in 52 weeks. Lists of stocks making new highs and new lows are available for the NYSE, Nasdaq and Amex. As an indicator, new highs and new lows are usually shown as moving averages to smooth the results and are often plotted together for easy comparison.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-n#nyse_composite_index_nya)

NYSE Composite Index ($NYA)

The NYSE Composite Index ($NYA) is an index that measures the market value of all common stocks listed on the NYSE, adjusted to account for capitalization changes, new stocks added to the list and stocks removed from the list. Over 200 years old, the New York Stock Exchange is one of the oldest continuously operating exchanges in the world. It is associated with large-cap blue chip stocks and is often used as a surrogate for the market as a whole.

circle-info

[**Chart $NYA in GalleryView.**arrow-up-right](https://stockcharts.com/gallery?$nya)

[**N**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-n)

[**E**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-e)

[**J**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-j)

[**O**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-o)

[**T**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-t)

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-c#call)

Call

A call is an options contract to buy an underlying asset. A call buyer has the right, but not the obligation, to purchase an underlying asset such as a stock, exchange-traded fund, or futures contract at a specific price. A call seller has the obligation to supply the underlying asset at a specific price if the contract is “exercised” by the buyer. When this happens, the call seller is said to be “assigned.” You can buy options on an index but they don't have an underlying asset. Instead, index options are cash-settled. See our ChartSchool article [Introduction to Options](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/overview/options-trading/introduction-to-options)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-c#candlestick_chart)

Candlestick Chart

A form of Japanese charting that has become popular in the West. A narrow line (shadow) shows the day's price range. A wider body marks the area between the open and the close. If the close is above the open, the body is white (not filled); if the close is below the open, the body is black (filled). See our ChartSchool article on [Introduction to Candlesticks](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/chart-analysis/candlestick-charts/introduction-to-candlesticks)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-c#capital_gain)

Capital Gain

The profit derived from the selling price exceeding its initial purchase price. A realized capital gain is an investment that has been sold at a profit. An unrealized capital gain is an investment that hasn't been sold yet but would result in a profit if sold. Capital gain is often used to mean realized capital gain.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-c#cash-secured-put)

Cash-Secured Put

A cash-secured put is when you sell a put option on an underlying asset and set aside enough cash in your brokerage account to buy the stock in the event you are assigned. See our ChartSchool article on [Options Trading Strategies](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/overview/options-trading/options-trading-strategies)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-c#cboe)

Cboe

The Chicago Board Options Exchange (CBOE). The largest options exchange in the US. The CBOE trades options for over 2000 equities, over 20 stock indices and over 100 ETFs.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-c#cboe_nasdaq_100_volatility_index_vxn)

Cboe Nasdaq 100 Volatility Index ($VXN)

Index representing the [implied volatility](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-i#implied_volatility)
 of a hypothetical 30-day option that is at the money, derived from a basket of put and call options. [Chart $VXN in GalleryView.arrow-up-right](https://stockcharts.com/gallery?$vxn)

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-c#chaikin_money_flow_cmf)

Chaikin Money Flow (CMF)

An oscillator that helps signal if a stock is undergoing accumulation or distribution. It is calculated from the daily readings of the [Accumulation Distribution Line](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-a#accumulation_distribution_line)
. The CMF is different from momentum oscillators in that it is not influenced by the daily price change; instead, the indicator focuses on the location of the close relative to the range for the period (daily or weekly). See our ChartSchool article on [Chaikin Money Flow](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/technical-indicators-and-overlays/technical-indicators/chaikin-money-flow-cmf)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-c#chaikin_oscillator)

Chaikin Oscillator

This is a moving average of the [Accumulation Distribution Line](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-a#accumulation_distribution_line)
. It was developed by Marc Chaikin. It is created by subtracting a 10-period exponential moving average of the accumulation/distribution line from a 3-period exponential average of it. See our ChartSchool article on [Chaikin Oscillator](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/technical-indicators-and-overlays/technical-indicators/chaikin-oscillator)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-c#chande_trend_meter_ctm)

Chande Trend Meter (CTM)

An indicator developed by Tushar Chande, which assigns a numerical score measuring a stock's trend strength. The scoring calculations are based on several technical indicators covering six different timeframes. CTM is plotted on a vertical scale from 0 to 100. Values above 80 are considered to be in a strong uptrend. See our ChartSchool article on [Chande Trend Meter](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/technical-indicators-and-overlays/technical-indicators/chande-trend-meter-ctm)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-c#chandelier_exit)

Chandelier Exit

An indicator that sets a trailing stop-loss based on the Average True Range (ATR). The Chandelier Exit is designed to keep traders in a trend and prevent an early exit as long as the trend extends. The indicator line will typically be above prices during a downtrend and below prices during an uptrend. See our ChartSchool article on [Chandelier Exit](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/technical-indicators-and-overlays/technical-overlays/chandelier-exit)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-c#channel)

Channel

When prices trend between two parallel trend lines, this is referred to as a channel. See our ChartSchool article on [Price Channel (Continuation)](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/chart-analysis/chart-patterns/price-channel)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-c#channel_line)

Channel Line

A straight line drawn parallel to the basic trend line. In an uptrend, the channel line slants up to the right and is drawn above rally peaks; in a downtrend, the channel line is drawn below price troughs and slants down to the right. Prices often meet resistance at rising channel lines and support at falling channel lines. See our ChartSchool article on [Price Channel (Continuation)](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/chart-analysis/chart-patterns/price-channel)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-c#commodities)

Commodities

Raw materials such as gold, pork bellies or orange juice. Traders in commodities buy and sell contracts (also called futures) for such materials.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-c#commodity_channel_index_cci)

Commodity Channel Index (CCI)

Developed by Donald Lambert, the CCI is an indicator designed to identify cyclical turns in commodities. It may also be applied to stocks or bonds. See our ChartSchool article on [Commodity Channel Index](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/technical-indicators-and-overlays/technical-indicators/commodity-channel-index-cci)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-c#confirmation)

Confirmation

A subsequent signal that validates a position stance. Traders and investors sometimes look for more than one signal or require validation before acting. For example, confirmation of a trend change may entail an advance past the previous reaction high. For an indicator such as MACD, confirmation of a divergence may be a subsequent moving average crossover.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-c#connorsrsi)

ConnorsRSI

ConnorsRSI is a momentum oscillator developed by Larry Connors and the team at Connors Research. It's used for identifying overbought/oversold conditions in shorter trading timeframes. ConnorsRSI combines the momentum measurement of RSI with components that measure the duration of the trend and the magnitude of the price change, to create a more reliable short-term RSI indicator. See our ChartSchool article on [ConnorsRSI](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/technical-indicators-and-overlays/technical-indicators/connorsrsi)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-c#contingent_deferred_sales_charge_cdsc)

Contingent Deferred Sales Charge (CDSC)

A form of commission that is a back-end load on mutual funds that decline over time. For instance, if you sell mutual fund shares that have a CDSC after one year, you may owe a 4% charge, but if you hold for three years, the charge may decline to 2%.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-c#continuation_pattern)

Continuation Pattern

A type of chart pattern that occurs in the middle of an existing trend. The previous trend resumes when the pattern is complete. Examples include the [Rectangle](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/chart-analysis/chart-patterns/rectangle)
 and [Pennant](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/chart-analysis/chart-patterns/flag-pennant)
 continuation patterns. For more continuation patterns, see our ChartSchool article on [Chart Patterns.](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/chart-analysis/chart-patterns)

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-c#contrarian)

Contrarian

A trading or investing style based on sentiment. Contrarians are typically bullish when sentiment is excessively bearish. Contrarians are typically bearish when sentiment is excessively bullish. The [Put/Call Ratio](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/market-indicators/put-call-ratio)
 is sentiment indicator used by contrarians.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-c#correction)

Correction

After an advance, a decline that does not penetrate the low from which the advance began is known as a correction. Also referred to as a retracement, a correction usually retraces 1/3 to 2/3 of the previous advance.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-c#correlation)

Correlation

Correlation measures the degree to which two securities move together. Positive correlation values indicate movement together in the same direction. Negative correlation values indicate movement in opposite directions. Correlation can be used to construct a well-diversified portfolio. See our ChartSchool article on [Correlation](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/overview/why-and-how-to-use-correlation)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-c#correlation_coefficient)

Correlation Coefficient

The Correlation Coefficient is a statistical measure that reflects the correlation between two securities. Positive correlation indicates that both are moving in the same direction; negative correlation indicates they are moving in opposite directions. Investors can use this indicator to identify securities with a low or negative correlation to the stock market, in order to diversify their holdings. See our ChartSchool article on the [Correlation Coefficient](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/technical-indicators-and-overlays/technical-indicators/correlation-coefficient)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-c#covered-call)

Covered Call

An options trading strategy that involves selling a call on a stock you already own. In other words, you're selling the right to buy a stock that you own at a specific price by a certain time. See our ChartSchool article on [Options Trading Strategies](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/overview/options-trading/options-trading-strategies)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-c#crb_index)

CRB Index

An unweighted geometric average of some important commodities, it averages prices across 17 commodities and across time. The index tracks energy, grains, industrials, livestock, precious metals and agriculturals. [Chart $CRB in GalleryView.arrow-up-right](https://stockcharts.com/gallery?$crb)

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-c#crossover)

Crossover

A point on a graph where two lines intersect. Depending on which lines they are, a crossover may indicate a buy or sell signal. For example, the price line crossing above a moving average line may generate a buy signal. Oscillators such as MACD and Chaikin Money Flow experience centerline crossovers. See our ChartSchool article on [Centerline Crossovers](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/technical-indicators-and-overlays/introduction-to-technical-indicators-and-oscillators#centerline_crossovers)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-c#cumulative)

Cumulative

A running total of values used to form an indicator. An example is [On Balance Volume](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/technical-indicators-and-overlays/technical-indicators/on-balance-volume-obv)
, which is a running total of volume. Volume is added on up days and subtracted on down days. See our ChartSchool article on the [AD Line](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/market-indicators/advance-decline-percent)
 for another example.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-c#cup_with_handle)

Cup with Handle

A bullish chart pattern that marks a consolidation period followed by a breakout. The “cup” part of the pattern resembles a rounding bottom, which is followed by a “handle” that acts as a final consolidation before a breakout. See our ChartSchool article on [Cup With Handle (Continuation)](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/chart-analysis/chart-patterns/cup-with-handle)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-c#cycles)

Cycles

A price high or low that repeats itself at the same interval over time. Cycle theory asserts that cyclical forces, both long and short, drive price movement in the financial markets. See our ChartSchool article on [Cycle Lines](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/chart-analysis/chart-annotation-tools/stock-market-cycles)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-c#cyclical_stocks)

Cyclical Stocks

Shares of companies that are highly sensitive to economic performance. Cyclical stocks tend to perform well when the economy is growing and suffer when the economy contracts. Chemical (Dupont), transportation (FDX Corp), auto (General Motors), paper (International Paper) and steel (Nucor) represent a few cyclical industries.

[PreviousGlossary - Bchevron-left](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-b)
[NextGlossary - Dchevron-right](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-d)

Last updated 5 months ago

# Glossary - O | ChartSchool | StockCharts.com

![Page cover](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/~gitbook/image?url=https%3A%2F%2F436553459-files.gitbook.io%2F%7E%2Ffiles%2Fv0%2Fb%2Fgitbook-x-prod.appspot.com%2Fo%2Fspaces%252FERtrZrZOhufFzk6ZQO4B%252Fuploads%252FyfwEW0v1wbvtlEYsoivr%252FGlossaryImage-2.jpg%3Falt%3Dmedia%26token%3D6fecd531-7f8d-4a18-a7e2-77302414e7e6&width=1248&dpr=4&quality=100&sign=ef9a26e6&sv=2)

[**A**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-a)

[**F**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-f)

[**K**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-k)

[**P**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-p)

[**U**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-u)

[**B**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-b)

[**G**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-g)

[**L**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-l)

[**Q**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-q)

[**V**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-v)

[**C**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-c)

[**H**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-h)

[**M**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-m)

[**R**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-r)

[**W**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-w)

[**D**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-d)

[**I**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-i)

[**N**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-n)

[**S**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-s)

[**X, Y, Z**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-x-y-z)

[**E**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-e)

[**J**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-j)

[**O**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-o)

[**T**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-t)

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-o#offer)

Offer

See [Ask](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-a#ask)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-o#on_balance_volume_obv)

On Balance Volume (OBV)

One of the first and most popular indicators to measure positive and negative volume flow, the OBV was introduced by Joe Granville in 1963. The concept behind the indicator is that volume precedes price. OBV is a simple indicator that adds a period's volume when the close is up and subtracts the period's volume when the close is down. A cumulative total of the volume additions and subtractions forms the OBV line. This line can then be compared with the price chart of the underlying security to look for [divergences](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-d#divergence)
 or [confirmation](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-c#confirmation)
. See our ChartSchool article on [On Balance Volume (OBV)](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/technical-indicators-and-overlays/technical-indicators/on-balance-volume-obv)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-o#open_interest)

Open Interest

The number of options or futures contracts that are still unliquidated at the end of a trading day. A rise or fall in open interest shows that money is flowing into or out of a futures contract or option, respectively. In futures markets, rising open interest is considered good for the current trend. Open interest also measures liquidity. See our ChartSchool article on [Options Pricing and Options Chains](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/overview/options-trading/options-pricing-and-options-chains)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-o#options-expiration)

Options Expiration

The options expiration date of an options contract is the last day you can exercise or trade the contract. Although most options contracts expire at the close of the expiration day, some expire in the morning. Be sure to check the exact time of expiration before you trade an options contract. See our ChartSchool article [Introduction to Options](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/overview/options-trading/introduction-to-options)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-o#oscillator)

Oscillator

An indicator that determines when a market is in an overbought or oversold condition. When the oscillator reaches an upper extreme, the market is overbought. When the oscillator line reaches a lower extreme, the market is oversold. See our ChartSchool article on [Oscillators](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/technical-indicators-and-overlays/introduction-to-technical-indicators-and-oscillators#oscillator_types)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-o#out-of-the-money-otm)

Out of the Money (OTM)

A call option that has a strike price that's higher than the prevailing price of the underlying asset is OTM. A put option with a strike price that's lower than the prevailing price of the underlying asset is OTM. See our ChartSchool article on [Options Pricing and Options Chains](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/overview/options-trading/options-pricing-and-options-chains)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-o#outside_day)

Outside Day

A chart pattern where the high is above the previous day's high and the low is below the previous day's low.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-o#outside_reversal_day)

Outside Reversal Day

A two-day chart pattern that can indicate a reversal in trend. Like with [outside days](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-o#outside_day)
, the second day's high must be above the previous day's high, while the low must be below the previous day's low. In addition, the second day must close in the opposite direction of the previous trend (e.g. in an uptrend, the second day must close down). This pattern is similar to the [engulfing candlestick pattern](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-e#engulfing_pattern)
, but requires the entire range (high and low) to be engulfed, not just the open and close.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-o#over_the_counter_otc)

Over The Counter (OTC)

A securities market that is not geographically centralized like the trading floor of the NYSE. OTC securities are traded through a telephone and computer network.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-o#overbought)

Overbought

A technical condition that occurs when prices are considered too high and susceptible to a decline. Overbought conditions can be classified by analyzing the chart pattern or with indicators such as the Stochastic Oscillator and Relative Strength Index (RSI). A sharp advance from $15 to $30 in 2 weeks might lead a technician to believe that a security is overbought. Sometimes a security is considered overbought when the Stochastic Oscillator exceeds 80 and when the Relative Strength Index (RSI) exceeds 70. It is important to keep in mind that overbought is not necessarily the same as being bearish. It merely infers that the stock has risen too far too fast and might be due for a pullback.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-o#oversold)

Oversold

A technical condition that occurs when prices are considered too low and ripe for a rally. Oversold conditions can be classified by analyzing the chart pattern or with indicators such as the Stochastic Oscillator and Relative Strength Index (RSI). A sharp decline from 30 to 15 in 2 weeks might lead a technician to believe that a security is oversold. Sometimes a security is considered oversold when the Stochastic Oscillator is less than 20 and when the Relative Strength Index (RSI) is less than 30. It is important to keep in mind that oversold is not necessarily the same as being bullish. It merely infers that the security has fallen too far too fast and may be due for a reaction rally.

[PreviousGlossary - Nchevron-left](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-n)
[NextGlossary - Pchevron-right](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-p)

Last updated 5 months ago

# Glossary - V | ChartSchool | StockCharts.com

![Page cover](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/~gitbook/image?url=https%3A%2F%2F436553459-files.gitbook.io%2F%7E%2Ffiles%2Fv0%2Fb%2Fgitbook-x-prod.appspot.com%2Fo%2Fspaces%252FERtrZrZOhufFzk6ZQO4B%252Fuploads%252FyfwEW0v1wbvtlEYsoivr%252FGlossaryImage-2.jpg%3Falt%3Dmedia%26token%3D6fecd531-7f8d-4a18-a7e2-77302414e7e6&width=1248&dpr=4&quality=100&sign=ef9a26e6&sv=2)

[**A**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-a)

[**F**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-f)

[**K**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-k)

[**P**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-p)

[**U**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-u)

[**B**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-b)

[**G**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-g)

[**L**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-l)

[**Q**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-q)

[**V**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-v)

[**C**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-c)

[**H**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-h)

[**M**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-m)

[**R**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-r)

[**W**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-w)

[**D**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-d)

[**I**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-i)

[**N**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-n)

[**S**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-s)

[**X, Y, Z**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-x-y-z)

[**E**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-e)

[**J**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-j)

[**O**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-o)

[**T**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-t)

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-v#value_line_arithmetic_composite_average_vle)

Value Line Arithmetic Composite Average ($VLE)

The Value Line Arithmetic Composite Average is an equally weighted price index of all stocks in the Value Line Investment Survey. It is referred to as “arithmetic” because the index is computed by finding the mean or simple average.

circle-info

[Chart $VLE in GalleryView.arrow-up-right](https://stockcharts.com/gallery?$vle)

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-v#value_line_geometric_composite_average_xvg)

Value Line Geometric Composite Average ($XVG)

The Value Line Geometric Composite Average is an equally weighted price index of all stocks in the Value Line Investment Survey. It is referred to as “geometric” because the index is computed by finding the geometric average. A geometric average is calculated by taking the nth root of the product of n terms.

circle-info

[Chart $XVG in GalleryView.arrow-up-right](https://stockcharts.com/gallery?$xvg)

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-v#vega)

Vega

The vega of an option measures the change in the price of the option relative to a 1% change in implied volatility. See our ChartSchool article on [Options Pricing and Options Chains](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/overview/options-trading/options-pricing-and-options-chains)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-v#vertical-spread)

Vertical Spread

A vertical spread options trading strategy comprises a long option and a short option—both must be calls or puts. In addition, both contracts have to be of the same quantity and expiration. There are different types of vertical spreads, depending on whether you're buying and selling calls or puts. See our ChartSchool article on [Options Trading Strategies](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/overview/options-trading/options-trading-strategies)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-v#visual_analysis)

Visual Analysis

A form of analysis that utilizes charts and market indicators to determine market direction.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-v#volatility)

Volatility

A measurement of change in price over a given period. It is usually expressed as a percentage and computed as the annualized standard deviation of the percentage change in daily price. The more volatile a stock or market, the more money an investor can gain (or lose!) in a short time.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-v#volatility_implied)

Volatility (Implied)

See [Implied Volatility (IV)](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-i#implied-volatility-iv)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-v#volatility_index_vix)

Volatility Index ($VIX)

The Market Volatility Index (VIX) measures the volatility of the market. A recent news story described it as “the options market's gauge of investor fear.” Traders use VIX as a general inverse indicator of market volatility and sentiment. High numbers mean that there's excess bearishness, while low numbers indicate excess bullishness. Created in 1993, the VIX is updated intraday by the Chicago Board Options Exchange (Cboe) using Standard & Poors 500 Index (SPX) bid/ask quotes. See our ChartSchool article on [Volatility Indices](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/market-indicators/volatility-indices)
 for more information.

circle-info

[Chart $VIX in GalleryView.arrow-up-right](https://stockcharts.com/gallery?$vix)

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-v#volume)

Volume

The number of trades in a security over a period of time. On a chart, volume is usually represented as a histogram (vertical bars) below the price chart. The NYSE and Nasdaq measure volume differently. For every buyer, there is a seller: 100 shares bought = 100 shares sold. The NYSE would count this as one trade and as 100 shares of volume. However, the Nasdaq would count each side of the trade and as 200 shares volume.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-v#volume_by_price)

Volume by Price

A horizontal histogram that overlays a price chart. The histogram bars stretch from left to right starting at the left side of the chart. The length of each bar is determined by the cumulative total of all volume bars for the periods during which the closing price fell within the vertical range of the histogram bar. See our ChartSchool article on [Volume by Price](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/technical-indicators-and-overlays/technical-overlays/volume-by-price)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-v#volume-weighted_average_price_vwap)

Volume-Weighted Average Price (VWAP)

An intraday indicator based on the total dollar value of all trades for the current day divided by the total trading volume for the current day. See our ChartSchool article on [VWAP](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/technical-indicators-and-overlays/technical-overlays/volume-weighted-average-price-vwap)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-v#volume_oscillator)

Volume Oscillator

See [Percentage Volume Oscillator (PVO)](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-p#percentage_volume_oscillator_pvo)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-v#vwap)

VWAP

See [Volume Weighted Average Price (VWAP)](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-v#volume-weighted_average_price_vwap)
.

# Glossary - T | ChartSchool | StockCharts.com

![Page cover](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/~gitbook/image?url=https%3A%2F%2F436553459-files.gitbook.io%2F%7E%2Ffiles%2Fv0%2Fb%2Fgitbook-x-prod.appspot.com%2Fo%2Fspaces%252FERtrZrZOhufFzk6ZQO4B%252Fuploads%252FyfwEW0v1wbvtlEYsoivr%252FGlossaryImage-2.jpg%3Falt%3Dmedia%26token%3D6fecd531-7f8d-4a18-a7e2-77302414e7e6&width=1248&dpr=4&quality=100&sign=ef9a26e6&sv=2)

[**A**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-a)

[**F**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-f)

[**K**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-k)

[**P**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-p)

[**U**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-u)

[**B**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-b)

[**G**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-g)

[**L**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-l)

[**Q**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-q)

[**V**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-v)

[**C**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-c)

[**H**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-h)

[**M**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-m)

[**R**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-r)

[**W**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-w)

[**D**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-d)

[**I**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-i)

[**N**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-n)

[**S**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-s)

[**X, Y, Z**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-x-y-z)

[**E**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-e)

[**J**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-j)

[**O**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-o)

[**T**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-t)

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-t#t-bill)

T-Bill

Also called Treasury Bills, these are short-term debt securities issued by the US government. Maturities are usually a year or less and typically run 13, 26 or 52 weeks. Trading in the secondary market, the price of T-Bills rises when interest rates fall and vice versa.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-t#technical_analysis)

Technical Analysis

The study of market action, usually with price charts, which includes volume and open interest patterns. Also called chart analysis, market analysis and, more recently, visual analysis. See our ChartSchool article on [Technical Analysis](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/overview/technical-analysis)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-t#ted_spread)

TED Spread

The difference between the three-month US Treasury rates and the three-month Eurodollar contract. Used to measure the strength of the credit markets.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-t#tema)

TEMA

See [Triple Exponential Moving Average (TEMA)](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-t#triple_exponential_moving_average_tema)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-t#theta)

Theta

The theta of an option represents time decay. As the expiration date of an option gets closer, the option's value decreases. Theta represents how much the price of an option decays as time passes one calendar day. See our ChartSchool article on [Options Pricing and Options Chains](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/overview/options-trading/options-pricing-and-options-chains)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-t#three_black_crows)

Three Black Crows

A bearish reversal pattern consisting of three consecutive black bodies, where each day closes at or near its low and opens within the body of the previous day.

![](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/~gitbook/image?url=https%3A%2F%2F436553459-files.gitbook.io%2F%7E%2Ffiles%2Fv0%2Fb%2Fgitbook-x-prod.appspot.com%2Fo%2Fspaces%252FERtrZrZOhufFzk6ZQO4B%252Fuploads%252F9fexttlzl3gHnUkb9vmf%252Fthreeblackcrows.gif%3Falt%3Dmedia%26token%3D5d1f8ac6-1517-4883-b3f3-953c8ae9cf4c&width=768&dpr=4&quality=100&sign=8e885037&sv=2)

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-t#three_white_soldiers)

Three White Soldiers

A bullish reversal pattern consisting of three consecutive white bodies, each with a higher close. Each should open within the previous body and the close should be near the high of the day.

![](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/~gitbook/image?url=https%3A%2F%2F436553459-files.gitbook.io%2F%7E%2Ffiles%2Fv0%2Fb%2Fgitbook-x-prod.appspot.com%2Fo%2Fspaces%252FERtrZrZOhufFzk6ZQO4B%252Fuploads%252FoKptF9hUXu7q0U1QiNCW%252Fthreewhitesoldiers.gif%3Falt%3Dmedia%26token%3Dd52a241b-cd31-4316-a1da-dac0288309e1&width=768&dpr=4&quality=100&sign=b5b0fe0&sv=2)

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-t#tick)

TICK

Each individual move from one stock trade to another. An UP-TICK means the price moved up on the last trade, while a DOWN-TICK means it moved down. If there is no change from the last trade, the TICK is considered neutral. The NYSE TICK indicator shows the net of all UP-TICKs minus all DOWN-TICKs on the NYSE exchange at a given point during the day. If 1000 stocks advanced on their last trade or TICK, 600 declined and 200 were unchanged, the TICK would be +400 (1000 minus 600 equals +400). The closing TICK is based on the last trade of the day. The TICK indicator is available for the NYSE and Nasdaq.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-t#top-down_approach)

Top-Down Approach

An approach to market analysis used by both fundamental and technical analysts. It often begins with a more “macro” analysis of the overall market through major indices (S&P 500, Dow, NYSE, etc.) before concentrating on the market at a more “micro” level. Strong and weak sectors of the market are analyzed before focusing on individual stocks within select groups.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-t#topping)

Topping

A period where the stock or market is “catching its breath” after an advance, characterized by a flat trading range without any noticeable trend. It is common to see a topping period after a lengthy increase of the stock price. Topping may be a sign of [distribution](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-d#distribution)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-t#trailing_stop)

Trailing Stop

A stop-loss level set above or below the current price that adjusts as the price fluctuates. For a long position, a trailing stop would be set below the current price and rise as the price advances. Should the price decline and reach the trailing stop, then a stop-loss would be triggered and the position closed. As long as the price remains above the trailing stop, the position is held. Indicators such as the [Parabolic SAR](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-p#parabolic_sar)
 or moving averages can be used to set trailing stops.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-t#treasury_bill)

Treasury Bill

See [T-Bill](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-t#t-bill)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-t#treasury_bond)

Treasury Bond

A long-term debt security issued by the US government. Maturities are usually set between 20 and 30 years and typically run for either 20 or 30 years. The 10-year Treasury Note is the benchmark security for US debt. Trading in the secondary market, the price of the Treasury Bonds rises when interest rates fall and declines when interest rates rise.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-t#treasury_note)

Treasury Note

Medium-term debt securities issued by the US government. Maturities are usually issued for between 2 and 10 years and typically run for 2, 5 or 10 years. The yield of the 10-year Treasury Note is commonly used as the benchmark interest rate. Trading in the secondary market, the price of the Treasury Notes rises when interest rates fall and declines when interest rates rise.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-t#trend)

Trend

Refers to the direction of prices. Rising peaks and troughs constitute an uptrend; falling peaks and troughs constitute a downtrend. A trading range is characterized by horizontal peaks and troughs. Trends are generally classified as major (longer than a year), intermediate (one to six months) or minor (less than a month).

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-t#trend_lines)

Trend Lines

Straight lines drawn on a chart below reaction lows (in an uptrend) or above rally peaks (in a downtrend) that determine the steepness of the current trend. The breaking of a trend line usually signals a trend reversal. See our ChartSchool article on [Trend Lines](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/chart-analysis/trend-lines)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-t#triangles)

Triangles

Sideways price patterns in which prices fluctuate with converging trend lines. The three types of triangles are the [symmetrical](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-s#symmetrical_triangle)
, [ascending](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-a#ascending_triangle)
, and [descending](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-d#descending_triangle)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-t#trigger_line)

Trigger Line

See [Signal Line](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-s#signal_line)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-t#trin)

TRIN

See [Arms Index](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-a#arms_index_trin)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-t#triple_bottom_breakdown)

Triple Bottom Breakdown

A bearish Point & Figure chart pattern that forms when an O-Column breaks below the equal lows of the two prior O-Columns. See our ChartSchool article on the [P&F Triple Bottom Breakdown](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/chart-analysis/point-and-figure-charts/classic-patterns/p-and-f-bearish-breakdowns#triple_bottom_breakdown)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-t#triple_bottom_reversal)

Triple Bottom Reversal

A bullish reversal pattern typically associated with line and bar charts. The pattern forms with three equal lows, at least one intermittent high to mark resistance and a breakout above resistance. See our ChartSchool article on [Triple Bottom (Reversal)](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/chart-analysis/chart-patterns/triple-bottom-reversal)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-t#triple_exponential_moving_average_tema)

Triple Exponential Moving Average (TEMA)

TEMA is a faster moving average developed by Patrick Molloy. Its calculation uses single-, double-, and triple-smoothed EMAs to offset the lag found in traditional EMAs, creating a more responsive moving average for short-term traders. See our ChartSchool article on [TEMA](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/technical-indicators-and-overlays/technical-overlays/triple-exponential-moving-average-tema)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-t#triple_top_breakout)

Triple Top Breakout

A bullish Point & Figure chart pattern that forms when an X-Column breaks above the equal highs of the two prior X-Columns. See our ChartSchool article on the [P&F Triple Top Breakout](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/chart-analysis/point-and-figure-charts/classic-patterns/p-and-f-bullish-breakouts#triple_top_breakout)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-t#triple_top_reversal)

Triple Top Reversal

A bearish reversal pattern typically associated with line and bar charts. The pattern forms with three prominent peaks, at least one intermittent low to mark support and a break below support. See our ChartSchool article on [Triple Top (Reversal)](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/chart-analysis/chart-patterns/triple-top-reversal)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-t#trix)

TRIX

A momentum indicator showing the percent rate-of-change of a triple exponentially-smoothed moving average. Like other oscillators, TRIX oscillates around a zero line. Its triple exponential smoothing makes it an excellent filter of market noise and it functions well as a leading indicator of market trends. See our ChartSchool article on [TRIX](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/technical-indicators-and-overlays/technical-indicators/trix)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-t#ttm_squeeze)

TTM Squeeze

A volatility and momentum indicator used to identify periods of consolidation and indicate the likely direction of a resulting move. Volatility is measured by looking for a squeeze: trading days where the security's Bollinger Bands are completely enclosed within the security's Keltner Channels, indicating very low volatility. Once the Bollinger Bands expand and break outside the Keltner Channels, the squeeze has “fired” and the security is likely to make a big price move. The momentum component of the indicator helps to determine the likely direction of that move. See our ChartSchool article on [TTM Squeeze](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/technical-indicators-and-overlays/technical-indicators/ttm-squeeze)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-t#typical_price)

Typical Price

The average of the high, low and close. Typical Price = (High + Low + Close)/3.

[PreviousGlossary - Schevron-left](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-s)
[NextGlossary - Uchevron-right](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-u)

Last updated 5 months ago

# Glossary - S | ChartSchool | StockCharts.com

![Page cover](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/~gitbook/image?url=https%3A%2F%2F436553459-files.gitbook.io%2F%7E%2Ffiles%2Fv0%2Fb%2Fgitbook-x-prod.appspot.com%2Fo%2Fspaces%252FERtrZrZOhufFzk6ZQO4B%252Fuploads%252FyfwEW0v1wbvtlEYsoivr%252FGlossaryImage-2.jpg%3Falt%3Dmedia%26token%3D6fecd531-7f8d-4a18-a7e2-77302414e7e6&width=1248&dpr=4&quality=100&sign=ef9a26e6&sv=2)

[**A**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-a)

[**F**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-f)

[**K**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-k)

[**P**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-p)

[**U**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-u)

[**B**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-b)

[**G**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-g)

[**L**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-l)

[**Q**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-q)

[**V**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-v)

[**C**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-c)

[**H**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-h)

[**M**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-m)

[**R**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-r)

[**W**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-w)

[**D**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-d)

[**I**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-i)

[**N**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-n)

[**S**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-s)

[**X, Y, Z**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-x-y-z)

[**E**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-e)

[**J**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-j)

[**O**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-o)

[**T**](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-t)

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-s#s_p_100_index_oex)

S&P 100 Index ($OEX)

The S&P 100 Index ($OEX) is a market-capitalization weighted index consisting of 100 large blue chip stocks across various industries. Many fund managers use this index as a benchmark to measure the performance of large-cap stocks overall. The market capitalization of the companies in the S&P 100 varies considerably, but in general, large companies have a market cap greater than $5 billion dollars.

circle-info

[Chart $OEX in GalleryView.arrow-up-right](https://stockcharts.com/gallery?$oex)

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-s#s_p_large_cap_500_index_spx)

S&P Large Cap 500 Index ($SPX)

The Standard and Poors (S&P) Large Cap 500 Index ($SPX) lists the 500 largest “large-cap” stocks (stocks from major companies in various industries). In 1999, companies in the Index ranged from $500 million to $400 billion in market capitalization.

circle-info

[Chart $SPX in GalleryView.arrow-up-right](https://stockcharts.com/gallery?$spx)

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-s#s_p_mid_cap_400_index_mid)

S&P Mid Cap 400 Index ($MID)

The Standard and Poors (S&P) Mid Cap 400 Index ($MID) lists 400 mid-size companies in various industries. According to S&P, it is a market-value weighted index. In 1999, the companies listed in the S&P 400 ranged from around $200 million to $10 billion in market capitalization.

circle-info

[Chart $MID in GalleryView.arrow-up-right](https://stockcharts.com/gallery?$mid)

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-s#s_p_small_cap_600_index_sml)

S&P Small Cap 600 Index ($SML)

The Standard and Poors (S&P) Small Cap 600 Index ($SML) lists 600 small-cap stocks, chosen for market size, liquidity and ability to represent various industry groups. In 1999, the companies listed in the S&P 600 ranged from around $40 million to $2 billion in market capitalization.

circle-info

[Chart $SML in GalleryView.arrow-up-right](https://stockcharts.com/gallery?$sml)

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-s#scan)

Scan

A list of stocks, sorted and filtered according to criteria that vary with the scan. StockCharts.com has an array of [Predefined Scansarrow-up-right](https://stockcharts.com/def/servlet/SC.scan)
; users may also create their own custom scans.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-s#sector)

Sector

A group of companies that generate revenue in similar ways; this revenue tends to rise and fall with the economic cycle. Sectors are commonly broken down into smaller groups called industries. The sectors tracked by S&P are Materials, Energy, Financials, Industrials, Technology, Consumer Staples, Utilities, Healthcare, Consumer Discretionary, Real Estate, and Communication Services.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-s#securities_and_exchange_commission_sec)

Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC)

A federal agency created to regulate and monitor the securities industry. All U.S. companies with stock must abide by the SEC rules and regulations and are required to file quarterly status reports.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-s#sell_signal)

Sell Signal

A condition that indicates a good time to sell a stock. The exact circumstances of the signal will be determined by the indicator that an analyst is using. For example, it's often considered a sell signal when the [RSI](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-r#relative_strength_index_rsi)
 crosses down through the 50 level.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-s#selling_climax)

Selling Climax

See [Reversal Spike](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-r#reversal_spike)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-s#semi-logarithmic_percentage_scaling)

Semi-Logarithmic (Percentage) Scaling

See [Logarithmic](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-l#logarithmic_percentage_scaling)
 Scaling.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-s#sentiment_indicators)

Sentiment Indicators

Psychological indicators that attempt to measure the degree of bullishness or bearishness in a market. These are contrary indicators and are used in much the same fashion as overbought or oversold oscillators. Their greatest value is when they reach upper or lower extremes.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-s#shakeout)

Shakeout

A situation where many scared investors exit their positions due to unfavorable news or uncertainty regarding the stock or industry. The dot-com bust was characterized by numerous shakeouts causing many to abandon their dot-com positions, often at great losses.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-s#shooting_star)

Shooting Star

A single day pattern that can appear in an uptrend. It opens higher, trades much higher, then closes near its open. It looks just like the Inverted Hammer; however, it is bearish, rather than bullish.

![](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/~gitbook/image?url=https%3A%2F%2F436553459-files.gitbook.io%2F%7E%2Ffiles%2Fv0%2Fb%2Fgitbook-x-prod.appspot.com%2Fo%2Fspaces%252FERtrZrZOhufFzk6ZQO4B%252Fuploads%252FixImKKwI6akpVmLkxk43%252Fshootingstar.gif%3Falt%3Dmedia%26token%3Db739702d-f76e-445e-94e1-f03e93ad0467&width=768&dpr=4&quality=100&sign=4cee49&sv=2)

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-s#short_selling)

Short Selling

The process of selling a stock with the hope of buying it back at a lower price (sell high, buy low). Short sellers are bearish and believe the price will decline. Short selling involves borrowing stock (usually from the broker) to sell short and using margin to finance the borrowing. If the price of the stock in question advances too far, the short seller will receive a margin call and be required to put up more money. A short squeeze occurs when the price advances so fast that short sellers are forced to cover their positions (buy the stock back), which drives prices even higher.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-s#signal_line)

Signal Line

Also known as a “trigger line”, it is a moving average of another indicator that is used to generate simple buy and sell signals. Probably the most used signal line is the one that is built into the [MACD](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-m#macd_moving_average_convergence_divergence)
 Indicator display. The signal line is the exponential moving average of the MACD line. A buy signal is generated when the MACD line crosses above the signal line and a sell signal is generated when the MACD line crosses below the signal line.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-s#simple_moving_average)

Simple Moving Average

A moving average that gives equal weight to each day's price data.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-s#six-month_cycle)

Six-Month Cycle

A bullish cycle that extends from November to April and a bearish cycle that extends from May to October. Yale Hirsch, founder of the _Stock Trader's Almanac_, discovered the six-month cycle in 1986.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-s#size)

Size

The number of shares immediately available to buy (bid) or sell (ask). A bid of $54 with a size of 500 would indicate an order to buy 500 shares at $54. An ask of $55 with a size of 1000 would indicate an order to sell 1000 shares at $55.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-s#slope)

Slope

The rise-over-run of an invisible line that is the “best fit” line through the previous _n_ data points.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-s#spdr)

SPDR

Referred to as “spider,” it stands for Standard & Poor's Depositary Receipt. SPDRs have three letter symbols (e.g. SPY) and trade just like stocks on the AMEX. They offer a convenient means to buy and sell the S&P 500, S&P 400 and select S&P sectors.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-s#spinning_top)

Spinning Top

Candlestick lines that have small bodies with upper and lower shadows that exceed the length of the body. Spinning tops signal indecision.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-s#split)

Split

The division of a stock into multiple shares. In a 2-for-1 split, the stockholder's shares will double in quantity, though the value of each stock will be halved. A stock split is usually an attempt to make high stock prices seem more attractive to investors and generally occurs in the face of new highs.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-s#spread)

Spread

The difference between the bid and the ask. Generally speaking, more liquid (heavy volume) stocks usually have smaller bid/ask spreads. Less liquid stocks (light volume) usually have larger spreads. See related: [bid](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-b#bid)
, [ask](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-a#ask)
, and [size](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-s#size)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-s#spring)

Spring

A situation that occurs when prices break below [support](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-s#support)
 but soon reverse course and move back above support. Prices are said to “spring” back from their support break and indicate that the bulls are still alive. A spring can also be referred to as a failed (bearish) signal and is considered bullish. Generally, the reversal should occur within 1-3 days of the support break for the failed signal to be considered valid. This is the opposite of an [upthrust](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-u#upthrust)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-s#standard_deviation_volatility)

Standard Deviation (Volatility)

A statistical term that provides a good indication of [volatility](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-v#volatility)
. It measures how widely values (closing prices for instance) are dispersed from the average. The larger the difference between the closing prices and the average price, the higher the standard deviation will be and the higher the volatility. The closer the closing prices are to the average price, the lower the standard deviation and the lower the volatility. See our ChartSchool article on [Standard Deviation (Volatility)](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/technical-indicators-and-overlays/technical-indicators/standard-deviation-volatility)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-s#stochastic_oscillator)

Stochastic Oscillator

A momentum indicator developed by George Lane that measures the price of a security relative to the high/low range over a set period. The indicator oscillates between 0 and 100, with readings below 20 considered oversold and readings above 80 considered overbought. A 14-period Stochastic Oscillator reading of 30 would indicate that the current price was 30% above the lowest low of the last 14 days and 70% below the highest high. The Stochastic Oscillator can be used like any other oscillator by looking for overbought/oversold readings, positive/negative divergences and centerline crossovers. See our ChartSchool article on [Stochastic Oscillator](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/technical-indicators-and-overlays/technical-indicators/stochastic-oscillator-fast-slow-and-full)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-s#stochrsi)

StochRSI

An oscillator used to identify overbought and oversold readings in [RSI](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-r#relative_strength_index_rsi)
. Because RSI can go for extended periods without becoming overbought (above 70) or oversold (below 30), StochRSI provides an alternative means to identify these extremities. StochRSI is found by applying the Stochastics formula to RSI readings – hence its name. As an indicator of RSI, it measures the value of RSI relative to its high/low range over a set number of periods. When RSI records a new low for the set period, StochRSI will be at 0. When RSI records a new high for the set period, StochRSI will be at 100. See our ChartSchool article on [StochRSI](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/technical-indicators-and-overlays/technical-indicators/stochrsi)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-s#stocks_above_their_200-day_moving_average)

Stocks Above Their 200-day Moving Average

A market breadth indicator that represents the number of stocks in a given group that have closing prices that are currently above their 200-day simple moving average. Common techniques for using this indicator include locating overbought/oversold levels and finding positive or negative divergences between this indicator and the underlying group's composite index.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-s#stocks_above_their_50-day_moving_average)

Stocks Above Their 50-day Moving Average

A market breadth indicator that represents the number of stocks in a given group that have closing prices that are currently above their 50-day simple moving average. Common techniques for using this indicator include locating overbought/oversold levels and finding positive or negative divergences between this indicator and the underlying group's composite index.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-s#stop_loss_order)

Stop Loss Order

An instruction to the broker to buy or sell stock when it trades beyond a specified price. They serve to either protect your profits or limit your losses.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-s#stop-and-reversal_indicator)

Stop-And-Reversal Indicator

See [Parabolic SAR](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-p#parabolic_sar)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-s#straddle)

Straddle

A straddle is an options strategy that involves buying a call and a put of the same strike price and expiration date. See our ChartSchool article on [Options Trading Strategies](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/overview/options-trading/options-trading-strategies)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-s#strangle)

Strangle

A strangle is an options strategy that involves buying an out-of-the-money (OTM) call and OTM put. Both contracts have the same expiration date but different strike prices. See our ChartSchool article on [Options Trading Strategies](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/overview/options-trading/options-trading-strategies)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-s#strike-price)

Strike Price

The price at which an option holder has the right to buy or sell the underlying security or the price at which an option can be exercised. See our ChartSchool article [Introduction to Options](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/overview/options-trading/introduction-to-options)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-s#summation_index)

Summation Index

See [McClellan Summation Index](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-m#mcclellan_summation_index)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-s#support)

Support

A price level at which there is sufficient demand for a stock to cause a halt in a downward trend and turn the trend up. Support levels indicate the price at which most investors feel that prices will move higher. See our ChartSchool article on [Support and Resistance](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/chart-analysis/support-and-resistance)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-s#swing_charting)

Swing Charting

A concept based on the use of a filter. Once prices have moved by the distance specified by this filter, a new line is drawn next to the previous one. In a nutshell, it is a chart that shows up and down price movement of a minimum size regardless of the time it takes. See our ChartSchool article on [Swing Charting](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/trading-strategies-and-models/trading-strategies/swing-charting)
.

### 

[hashtag](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/glossary/glossary-s#symmetrical_triangle)

Symmetrical Triangle

A sideways chart pattern between two converging trend lines in which the upper trend line is declining and the lower trend line is rising. This pattern represents an even balance between buyers and sellers, although the prior trend is usually resumed. The breakout through either trend line signals the direction of the price trend. See our ChartSchool article on [Symmetrical Triangle (Continuation)](https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/chart-analysis/chart-patterns/symmetrical-triangle)
.
